text: Minimally invasive Ivor Lewis esophagectomy.
The laparoscopic camera provides a view of the abdominal cavity as the minimally invasive Ivor Lewis esophagectomy procedure begins.
The surgeon places a liver attractor and incises the gastropathic ligament, revealing underlying structures in the surgical field.
The surgeon continues dissection toward the esophageal hiatus, identifying the right crus of the diaphragm during the minimally invasive Ivor Lewis esophagectomy.
The surgeon incises the frontal esophageal ligament, continuing the dissection toward the esophageal hiatus.
The surgeon continues dissection through the esophageal hiatus into the mediastinum, advancing the minimally invasive Ivor Lewis esophagectomy procedure.
The surgeon retracts the esophagus anteriorly, revealing the left crus of the diaphragm within the mediastinum.
The surgeon focuses on the greater curvature of the stomach, preparing for further dissection or manipulation in this area.
The surgeon incises the gastrocolic ligament, providing access to the lesser sac, with visible separation of the tissue layers.
The surgeon carefully identifies and preserves the right gastroepiploic artery and vein during the dissection, ensuring these structures remain intact.
The surgeon dissects toward the fundus of the stomach, dividing the short gastric arteries and veins using a bipolar device.
The surgeon divides the loose attachments between the stomach and pancreas, separating these structures to facilitate further dissection.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the stomach, detaching it from the retroperitoneum, revealing the fully freed stomach structure.
The surgeon reflects the stomach anteriorly, revealing the left gastric pedicle for identification.
The surgeon sweeps lymph nodes along the left gastric pedicle upward toward the specimen, ensuring they are included in the dissection.
The surgeon divides the left gastric artery and vein using a linear stapler, completing the separation of these vessels from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon performs a standard pyloroplasty incision on the stomach, following its complete mobilization and separation from the pancreas.
The surgeon divides the pyloric ring and closes the incision transversely, completing the pyloroplasty as part of the esophagectomy procedure.
The surgeon meticulously incorporates the mucosa into each stitch during the pyloroplasty, ensuring precise alignment and secure closure of the incision.
The surgeon places four to five sutures to close the pyloroplasty incision, ensuring the closure is secure and complete.
The surgeon begins the creation of the gastric tube, preparing the stomach for reconstruction.
The surgeon uses a vascular stapler to divide the vessels along the lesser curvature of the stomach, preparing the area for further surgical manipulation.
The surgeon fashions the gastric tube by sequentially firing a linear stapler along the stomach, shaping it into a tube-like structure.
The surgeon measures the width of the gastric tube, which is approximately five centimeters, ensuring it is appropriately sized for the procedure.
The surgeon applies traction on the fundus of the stomach, pulling it toward the left hemidiaphragm to facilitate the next step in the procedure.
The surgeon applies the stapler to the gastric tube, ensuring proper alignment to prevent spiraling during the creation of the tube.
After creating the gastric tube, the surgeon places a single stitch to connect the specimen to the conduit.
The surgeon pulls both the esophageal specimen and the gastric conduit into the chest cavity after mobilizing the esophagus during thoracoscopy.
The surgeon examines the esophageal hiatus, inspecting the anatomical area where the esophagus passes through the diaphragm.
A single stitch is placed to partially close the esophageal hiatus, visible at the junction where the esophagus passes through the diaphragm.
This was done to minimize the risk of delayed herniation of abdominal contents across the diaphragm into the chest.
The surgeon initiates thoracoscopy, providing a visual view inside the chest cavity to continue the esophagectomy procedure.
The surgeon places a suture in the central tendon of the diaphragm, securing the area during the esophagectomy procedure.
The surgeon brings the suture out through a separate small incision in the chest wall, visible as the suture exits the thoracic cavity.
The surgeon applies downward traction on the suture, providing clear visualization of the distal thoracic esophagus within the inferior pulmonary ligament.
The surgeon uses a fan retractor to reflect the lung anteriorly, providing clear access to the distal thoracic esophagus and surrounding structures.
The surgeon divides the inferior pulmonary ligament using an ultrasonic device, separating the ligament from surrounding structures in the thoracic cavity.
The surgeon carries the division of the mediastinal pleura superiorly, approaching the level of the azygos vein, with visible separation of the pleural tissue.
The surgeon identifies the azagus vein and carefully mobilizes it circumferentially, ensuring clear exposure and separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon divides the azagus vein using a vascular stapler, completing the mobilization process initiated earlier.
The surgeon identifies and divides the right vagus nerve, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon sweeps peri-esophageal lymph nodes off the pericardium, clearing the area around the esophagus.
The surgeon continues the dissection laterally, approaching the border of the right main stem bronchus, maintaining the established plane.
The surgeon skeletonizes the right main stem bronchus and performs a subcarinal lymph node dissection, moving superiorly toward the carina.
The surgeon carefully identifies the right main stem bronchus to avoid injuring the posterior membranous portion of the airway during the procedure.
The surgeon skeletonizes the left main stem bronchus, completing the subcarinal nodal dissection, with visible separation of the bronchus from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon incises the mediastinal pleura located posterior to the esophagus, revealing underlying structures in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully identifies the thoracic duct while preserving the overlying mediastinal pleura during the esophagectomy procedure.
The surgeon identifies and divides small branches of the thoracic duct and aortoesophageal branches between surgical clips.
The surgeon mobilizes the esophagus towards the thoracic inlet, carefully manipulating the tissue to ensure proper positioning.
The surgeon dissects above the azygos vein, maintaining the dissection plane close to the esophageal wall, ensuring precise separation of the esophagus from surrounding structures.
This will minimize injury to the recurrent laryngeal nerve.
The surgeon pulls the gastric specimen and chondroit into the mediastinum, positioning them within the thoracic cavity.
The surgeon divides the esophagus just above the level of the azygos vein, completing the separation of the esophagus in this region.
The surgeon enlarges the posterior port site and inserts a laparoscopic wound protector to safeguard the incision area during the procedure.
The surgeon extracts the specimen through the enlarged posterior port site, utilizing the laparoscopic wound protector for safe removal.
The surgeon places the anvil of a 28mm stapler into the chest cavity and inserts it into the lumen of the thoracic esophagus, preparing for anastomosis.
The surgeon secures the anvil in the thoracic esophagus using two concentric purse string sutures, ensuring it is firmly positioned for the subsequent stapling process.
The surgeon creates a gastronomy in the gastric conduit, positioned adjacent to the staple line along the lesser curvature of the stomach.
The surgeon introduces the stapler into the gastric conduit, preparing for the anastomosis between the gastric conduit and the thoracic esophagus.
The surgeon pulls the excess gastric conduit into the mediastinum in preparation for performing the anastomosis.
The stapler penetrates the greater curve of the stomach and docks onto the anvil, preparing for the anastomosis within the thoracic esophagus.
The surgeon fires the stapler, creating a circular esophagogastric anastomosis, visibly joining the esophagus and gastric conduit.
The surgeon closes the gastronomy in the gastric conduit using sequential firings of a linear stapler, ensuring the staple line is secure.
The surgeon irrigates the chest cavity and places a Jackson-Pratt drain and chest tube adjacent to the esophagogastric anastomosis site.
The surgeon re-expands the lung, marking the completion of the esophagectomy procedure.
